question:
There are 2010 boxes labeled $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_{2010}$, and $2010n$ balls have been distributed
among them, for some positive integer $n$. You may redistribute the balls by a sequence of moves,
each of which consists of choosing an $i$ and moving \emph{exactly} $i$ balls from box $B_i$ into any
one other box. For which values of $n$ is it possible to reach the distribution with exactly $n$ balls
in each box, regardless of the initial distribution of balls?
\medskip
solution:
It is possible if and only if $n \geq 1005$.
Since
\[
1 + \cdots + 2009 = \frac{2009 \times 2010}{2} = 2010 \times 1004.5,
\]
for $n \leq 1004$, we can start with an initial distribution in which each box
$B_i$ starts with at most $i-1$ balls (so in particular $B_1$ is empty).
From such a distribution, no moves are possible, so
we cannot reach the desired final distribution.

Suppose now that $n \geq 1005$.
By the pigeonhole principle, at any time, there exists at least one index $i$ for which the box $B_i$
contains at least $i$ balls. We will describe any such index as being \emph{eligible}.
The following sequence of operations then has the desired effect.
\begin{itemize}
\item[(a)]
Find the largest eligible index $i$.
If $i=1$, proceed to (b).
Otherwise, move $i$ balls from $B_i$ to $B_1$, then repeat (a).
\item[(b)]
At this point, only the index $i=1$ can be eligible (so it must be).
Find the largest index $j$ for which $B_j$ is nonempty.
If $j=1$, proceed to (c).
Otherwise, move 1 ball from $B_1$ to $B_j$; in case this makes $j$ eligible,
move $j$ balls from $B_j$ to $B_1$. Then repeat (b).
\item[(c)]
At this point, all of the balls are in $B_1$. For $i=2,\dots,2010$,
move one ball from $B_1$ to $B_i$ $n$ times.
\end{itemize}
After these operations, we have the desired distribution.

You may redistribute the balls by a sequence of moves, each of which consists of choosing an $indexvar$ and moving \emph{exactly} $indexvar$ balls from box $boxfori$ into any one other box. For which values of $perboxcount$ is it possible to reach the distribution with exactly $perboxcount$ balls in each box, regardless of the initial distribution of balls? \medskip

\[

\]
for $perboxcount \leq 1004$, we can start with an initial distribution in which each box

From such a distribution, no moves are possible, so

The following sequence of operations then has the desired effect.

\item[(a)]
Find the largest eligible index $indexvar$.

Otherwise, move $indexvar$ balls from $boxfori$ to $boxone$, then repeat (a).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
There are 2010 boxes labeled $labyrinth, silhouette, \dots, gemstone$, and $2010windchime$ balls have been distributed
among them, for some positive integer $windchime$. You may redistribute the balls by a sequence of moves,
each of which consists of choosing an $tangerine$ and moving \emph{exactly} $tangerine$ balls from box $buttercup$ into any
one other box. For which values of $windchime$ is it possible to reach the distribution with exactly $windchime$ balls
in each box, regardless of the initial distribution of balls?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
It is possible if and only if $windchime \geq 1005$.
Since
\[
1 + \cdots + 2009 = \frac{2009 \times 2010}{2} = 2010 \times 1004.5,
\]
for $windchime \leq 1004$, we can start with an initial distribution in which each box
$buttercup$ starts with at most $tangerine-1$ balls (so in particular $labyrinth$ is empty).
From such a distribution, no moves are possible, so
we cannot reach the desired final distribution.

Suppose now that $windchime \geq 1005$.
By the pigeonhole principle, at any time, there exists at least one index $tangerine$ for which the box $buttercup$
contains at least $tangerine$ balls. We will describe any such index as being \emph{eligible}.
The following sequence of operations then has the desired effect.
\begin{itemize}
\item[(a)]
Find the largest eligible index $tangerine$.
If $tangerine=1$, proceed to (b).
Otherwise, move $tangerine$ balls from $buttercup$ to $labyrinth$, then repeat (a).
\item[(b)]
At this point, only the index $tangerine=1$ can be eligible (so it must be).
Find the largest index $marzipan$ for which $dandelion$ is nonempty.
If $marzipan=1$, proceed to (c).
Otherwise, move 1 ball from $labyrinth$ to $dandelion$; in case this makes $marzipan$ eligible,
move $marzipan$ balls from $dandelion$ to $labyrinth$. Then repeat (b).
\item[(c)]
At this point, all of the balls are in $labyrinth$. For $tangerine=2,\dots,2010$,
move one ball from $labyrinth$ to $buttercup$ $windchime$ times.
\end{itemize}
After these operations, we have the desired distribution.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
There are 2010 boxes labeled $boundless, limitless, \dots, unbounded$, and $2010scarcity$ balls have been distributed
among them, for some positive integer $scarcity$. You may redistribute the balls by a sequence of moves,
each of which consists of choosing an $entirety$ and moving \emph{exactly} $entirety$ balls from box $emptiness$ into any
one other box. For which values of $scarcity$ is it possible to reach the distribution with exactly $scarcity$ balls
in each box, regardless of the initial distribution of balls?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
It is possible if and only if $scarcity \geq 1005$.
Since
\[
1 + \cdots + 2009 = \frac{2009 \times 2010}{2} = 2010 \times 1004.5,
\]
for $scarcity \leq 1004$, we can start with an initial distribution in which each box
$emptiness$ starts with at most $entirety-1$ balls (so in particular $boundless$ is empty).
From such a distribution, no moves are possible, so
we cannot reach the desired final distribution.

Suppose now that $scarcity \geq 1005$.
By the pigeonhole principle, at any time, there exists at least one index $entirety$ for which the box $emptiness$
contains at least $entirety$ balls. We will describe any such index as being \emph{eligible}.
The following sequence of operations then has the desired effect.
\begin{itemize}
\item[(a)]
Find the largest eligible index $entirety$.
If $entirety=1$, proceed to (b).
Otherwise, move $entirety$ balls from $emptiness$ to $boundless$, then repeat (a).
\item[(b)]
At this point, only the index $entirety=1$ can be eligible (so it must be).
Find the largest index $wholeness$ for which $fullness$ is nonempty.
If $wholeness=1$, proceed to (c).
Otherwise, move 1 ball from $boundless$ to $fullness$; in case this makes $wholeness$ eligible,
move $wholeness$ balls from $fullness$ to $boundless$. Then repeat (b).
\item[(c)]
At this point, all of the balls are in $boundless$. For $entirety=2,\dots,2010$,
move one ball from $boundless$ to $emptiness$ $scarcity$ times.
\end{itemize}
After these operations, we have the desired distribution.

garbled_string.question:
There are 2010 boxes labeled $qwertyui, zxcvbnml, \dots, poiuytre$, and $2010asdfghjk$ balls have been distributed
among them, for some positive integer $asdfghjk$. You may redistribute the balls by a sequence of moves,
each of which consists of choosing an $qzxwvtnp$ and moving \emph{exactly} $qzxwvtnp$ balls from box $mnpqrsuvw$ into any
one other box. For which values of $asdfghjk$ is it possible to reach the distribution with exactly $asdfghjk$ balls
in each box, regardless of the initial distribution of balls?
\medskip
garbled_string.solution:
It is possible if and only if $asdfghjk \geq 1005$.
Since
\[
1 + \cdots + 2009 = \frac{2009 \times 2010}{2} = 2010 \times 1004.5,
\]
for $asdfghjk \leq 1004$, we can start with an initial distribution in which each box $mnpqrsuvw$ starts with at most $qzxwvtnp-1$ balls (so in particular $qwertyui$ is empty).
From such a distribution, no moves are possible, so
we cannot reach the desired final distribution.

Suppose now that $asdfghjk \geq 1005$.
By the pigeonhole principle, at any time, there exists at least one index $qzxwvtnp$ for which the box $mnpqrsuvw$
contains at least $qzxwvtnp$ balls. We will describe any such index as being \emph{eligible}.
The following sequence of operations then has the desired effect.
\begin{itemize}
\item[(a)]
Find the largest eligible index $qzxwvtnp$.
If $qzxwvtnp=1$, proceed to (b).
Otherwise, move $qzxwvtnp$ balls from $mnpqrsuvw$ to $qwertyui$, then repeat (a).
\item[(b)]
At this point, only the index $qzxwvtnp=1$ can be eligible (so it must be).
Find the largest index $hjgrksla$ for which $lkjhgfdsa$ is nonempty.
If $hjgrksla=1$, proceed to (c).
Otherwise, move 1 ball from $qwertyui$ to $lkjhgfdsa$; in case this makes $hjgrksla$ eligible,
move $hjgrksla$ balls from $lkjhgfdsa$ to $qwertyui$. Then repeat (b).
\item[(c)]
At this point, all of the balls are in $qwertyui$. For $qzxwvtnp=2,\dots,2010$,
move one ball from $qwertyui$ to $mnpqrsuvw$ $asdfghjk$ times.
\end{itemize}
After these operations, we have the desired distribution.

kernel_variant.question: Let m = 2023.  There are 2023 boxes labelled B_1, B_2, \ldots  , B_{2023} and a total of 2023 n balls has been distributed among them, where n is a positive integer.  A legal move consists of choosing an index i (1 \leq  i \leq  2023) and moving exactly i balls from box B_i to some single *different* box.  For which values of n is it guaranteed that, regardless of the initial distribution of the 2023 n balls, one can reach the configuration in which every box contains exactly n balls?
kernel_variant.solution:
Answer.
It is possible to reach the uniform configuration (n, n, \ldots  , n) from every starting position if and only if n \geq  1012.

------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Necessity: failure for n \leq  1011.

Put S = 2023 n.  Let k be the largest integer for which k(k - 1)/2 \leq  S.  (Equivalently, k is the largest triangular number not exceeding S.)  When n \leq  1011 we have
   2023 \geq  k \geq  1,
and in fact k = 2023 exactly when n = 1011 (because 2023\cdot 2022/2 = 2023\cdot 1011 = S).

Write S = k(k - 1)/2 + r with 0 \leq  r < k.  Define an initial distribution as follows.
   * For i = 1, 2, \ldots  , k set B_i = i - 1.
   * If k < 2023 (this happens when n \leq  1010) put the remaining r balls into B_{k+1}; if k = 2023 (so r = 0) no extra box is needed.
   * All other boxes (if any) are left empty.

Clearly \Sigma B_i = S.  Moreover, every occupied box satisfies B_i \leq  i - 1 < i, so *no* box is eligible for a legal move.  Consequently no moves at all are possible and the uniform distribution cannot be reached.  Hence the task is impossible whenever n \leq  1011.

------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Sufficiency: success for n \geq  1012.

We describe a constructive algorithm that always terminates in the desired state.

Phase I - Collect surplus in B_1.
Call a box *eligible* if it contains at least as many balls as its index.  While there exists an eligible box with index i > 1, choose the *largest* such i and move i balls from B_i to B_1.  The move is legal, preserves the total number of balls, and strictly lowers B_i, so the process stops after finitely many steps.

When it stops, every box with index \geq  2 satisfies B_i \leq  i - 1, whence
      \Sigma _{i=2}^{2023} B_i \leq  1 + 2 + \cdots  + 2022 = 2023\cdot 1011.
Because n \geq  1012, the total 2023 n exceeds this bound, so B_1 = 2023 n - \Sigma _{i>1}B_i \geq  2023 \geq  1.  Thus B_1 itself is eligible.

Phase II - Empty the other boxes.
For j = 2, 3, \ldots  , 2023 perform the following loop until B_j = 0.
   * Move one ball from B_1 to B_j (legal because B_1 \geq  1).
   * If this makes B_j = j, immediately move j balls from B_j back to B_1 (legal because B_j now equals its index).  After that B_j returns to 0.
Throughout this phase B_1 never falls below 1, so every step is legal.  When the loop has run for every j \geq  2, *all* balls reside in B_1.

Phase III - Redistribute evenly.
For k = 2, 3, \ldots  , 2023 repeat the legal move ``transfer 1 ball from B_1 to B_k'' exactly n times.  In total 2022 n balls leave B_1, so B_1 is left with 2023 n - 2022 n = n balls, and each B_k (k \geq  2) also ends with n balls.  The uniform distribution has been reached.

------------------------------------------------------------
Since sufficiency holds for n \geq  1012 and we proved impossibility for n \leq  1011, the desired condition is
                            n \geq  1012 .

For n > (mŌłÆ1)/2, the pigeonhole principle guarantees an 'eligible' box (some i with at least i balls) at every stage.
Repeatedly move i balls from the largest eligible box to a chosen collector box (taken as BŌéü) until BŌéü is the only eligible one.
From BŌéü, send one ball at a time to the largest remaining non-empty box until it becomes eligible, then pull its i balls back to BŌéü; repeat until all balls are in BŌéü.
Distribute one ball from BŌéü to every other box exactly n times, giving n balls per box.
